mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter is six years old, and she won't say hello to anyone. i'm not exaggerating — she genuinely won't greet people, no matter who they are or how well she knows them. when we arrive at school every morning, her teacher greets her warmly — 'good morning, priya! how are you today?' — and my daughter just walks past like the teacher isn't even there. she doesn't make eye contact, doesn't wave, doesn't acknowledge the greeting at all. she just walks to her cubby as if no one spoke. when relatives come to visit — grandparents she loves, aunts and uncles she's known her whole life — they say hello and try to hug her, and she turns away or hides behind me. at birthday parties, when other kids run up and say hi, she stares at the floor or walks in the opposite direction. if someone waves at her in the grocery store, she looks through them. i've been prompting her since she could talk. every single greeting situation, i'm whispering in her ear — 'say hi to grandma,' 'wave to your teacher,' 'tell them your name.' sometimes, if i push hard enough, she'll whisper a barely audible 'hi' while looking at the ground. but she never, ever does it on her own. not once. not with anyone. i keep thinking she'll outgrow it, that one day it will click. but she's six now, and it's getting worse, not better. the gap between her and other kids her age is widening. they greet adults naturally, wave to friends, introduce themselves. she can't — or won't — do any of it. it's not just shyness. i know what shyness looks like. her younger brother is shy too, but he still waves and says hello with some encouragement. he warms up after a few minutes. my daughter doesn't warm up. she seems like she genuinely doesn't understand why she should greet people, or doesn't notice that a greeting moment is happening, or is so uncomfortable that she can't make herself do it even when she wants to. i don't know which one it is. maybe all three. people think she's rude. i can see it on their faces — the neighbor who waves enthusiastically and gets nothing back, the family friend who bends down to say hello and watches my daughter turn away. they think she's being impolite or that i haven't taught her manners. but i have. god, i have. i've taught her everything i can think of. family members are hurt. my mother has cried about it — 'why won't she say hello to me? does she not love me?' i can see teachers forming opinions. the notes from school mention that she 'struggles with social engagement' and 'rarely acknowledges peers or adults.' i'm exhausted from the constant prompting, the constant apologizing, the constant explaining that she's not rude, she's just... i don't even know how to explain it anymore. here's the thing that breaks my heart — at home, with just our family, she's sweet and loving and talkative. she'll tell me long stories about her day. she'll cuddle and laugh and engage. but the moment we step outside or someone new appears, it's like a wall goes up. she won't even look at people, let alone greet them. the world doesn't see who she really is because she can't get past the first moment of any social interaction. she won't say hello. and without that doorway, nothing else can happen. why doesn't my child greet people? why doesn't she understand this most basic social exchange? and how do i teach something that i always assumed just... happened naturally?

acceptedAnswer.text: GREETING SKILLS DEFICIT / SOCIAL INITIATION CHALLENGES / PRAGMATIC LANGUAGE DIFFICULTIES / SOCIAL COMMUNICATION DIFFERENCES / SOCIAL ANXIETY AFFECTING GREETINGS! Greeting — acknowledging another person's presence through words, gestures, or eye contact — is one of the foundational social communication skills. It serves as the gateway to social interaction, signaling recognition, openness to engagement, and basic social connection. While greeting feels automatic to most neurotypical individuals, it actually requires the integration of multiple skills: recognizing that a greeting situation is occurring, understanding the social expectation, initiating the appropriate verbal and nonverbal response, managing any discomfort around the interaction, and executing the greeting with appropriate timing and intensity. Children may struggle with greetings due to social communication differences (not recognizing or understanding the greeting ritual), anxiety (overwhelming discomfort in social moments), sensory challenges (eye contact or touch aversion), pragmatic language deficits (not knowing what to say or how), or simply never having explicitly learned the skill that others acquired implicitly. Effective intervention teaches greetings explicitly, provides practice in structured and naturalistic contexts, addresses underlying barriers, and builds the skill from recognition to independent initiation.

mainEntity.name: What is Greeting Skills Deficit / Social Initiation Challenges / Pragmatic Language Difficulties / Social Communication Differences / Social Anxiety Affecting Greetings?

acceptedAnswer.text: Greeting — acknowledging another person's presence through words, gestures, or eye contact — is one of the foundational social communication skills. It serves as the gateway to social interaction, signaling recognition, openness to engagement, and basic social connection. While greeting feels automatic to most neurotypical individuals, it actually requires the integration of multiple skills: recognizing that a greeting situation is occurring, understanding the social expectation, initiating the appropriate verbal and nonverbal response, managing any discomfort around the interaction, and executing the greeting with appropriate timing and intensity. Children may struggle with greetings due to social communication differences (not recognizing or understanding the greeting ritual), anxiety (overwhelming discomfort in social moments), sensory challenges (eye contact or touch aversion), pragmatic language deficits (not knowing what to say or how), or simply never having explicitly learned the skill that others acquired implicitly. Effective intervention teaches greetings explicitly, provides practice in structured and naturalistic contexts, addresses underlying barriers, and builds the skill from recognition to independent initiation. Common signs include: Ignores people who greet them, Walks past without acknowledging greetings, Looks away or at the floor when greeted, Hides behind parent when people say hello, Won't wave even when prompted.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories About Greetings help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social Stories® (developed by Carol Gray) explain social situations in clear, explicit terms from the child's perspective. For greetings, social stories explain what greetings are, why people use them, what happens when someone says hello, what the child can do in response, and how greetings make people feel. Many children who struggle with greetings have never had the implicit social knowledge made explicit — they've observed greetings but haven't understood their purpose or importance. Social stories fill this gap, building conceptual understanding before expecting behavioral change. They answer questions the child may not have known to ask: Why do people say hello? What does it mean? Why does it matter? What should I do when it happens? Reading stories repeatedly before greeting situations primes awareness and provides a framework for understanding what's about to occur. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Write personalized social stories using Carol Gray's framework. Include: descriptive sentences (When I see someone I know, they might say 'hello'), perspective sentences (They want me to know they see me and are happy to see me), directive sentences (I can wave, say 'hi,' or smile), affirmative sentences (This is called greeting, and it helps people feel connected). Use photos of the child and familiar people. Safety: Social stories should be read when calm, not in the greeting moment. They are preparation tools. Tone should be informative and supportive, never punitive.

mainEntity.name: How does a Greeting Picture Cards and Visual Sequences help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Greeting picture cards break down the greeting sequence into visible, discrete steps. Cards might show: 1) See person, 2) Look at them, 3) Wave or say hi, 4) Listen for their response. This visual breakdown transforms an overwhelming social moment into manageable, concrete steps. For children who freeze in greeting situations because they don't know what to do first, picture sequences provide a roadmap. Cards can be reviewed before greeting situations, practiced at home, and even carried as portable reminders. Visual sequences also help children who don't recognize when a greeting situation is occurring — the cards illustrate what a greeting looks like from the outside, helping children spot their cues. Different cards can show different greeting types: casual wave, verbal 'hi,' handshake for formal situations, or culturally specific greetings. Price: ₹150–600. DIY option: Create simple cards with photos or drawings showing each step. Use photos of the child demonstrating each step for personalization. Laminate for durability. Put on a ring for portable sequencing. Safety: Keep sequences simple — three to four steps maximum. Overwhelming detail creates more freeze, not less.

mainEntity.name: How does a Mirror Practice Materials help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mirror practice allows children to see themselves greeting — observing their own facial expressions, eye direction, and body language. Many children who struggle with greetings don't know what their greeting looks like from the outside. They may not realize they're looking at the floor, that their voice is too quiet, or that their wave is nearly invisible. Mirrors provide visual feedback that verbal descriptions cannot. Practice in front of a mirror also reduces the social pressure of practicing with real people — the child interacts only with their reflection, which doesn't judge or react unpredictably. This creates a low-stakes practice environment where children can try, adjust, and build confidence before greeting real people. Mirror practice builds the muscle memory and self-awareness that transfer to real greeting situations. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Any mirror works. Stand-alone mirrors at child height are ideal. Practice saying hello, waving, making eye contact. Record on phone for review. Make it playful — 'Let's practice our hellos!' Safety: Mirrors should be safely mounted or stable. Keep practice sessions short and positive. If the child shows distress seeing themselves, take a break.

mainEntity.name: How does a Puppets and Stuffed Animals for Role-Play help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Puppets and stuffed animals create safe, playful practice partners for greeting rehearsal. The child can practice greeting a puppet without the unpredictability of human responses. They can also use puppets to act out greeting scenarios, seeing the exchange from different perspectives. Puppets create psychological distance — it's the puppet greeting, not the child, which reduces pressure while building skill. Role-play with puppets can address specific scenarios: greeting at school, greeting family, greeting strangers, handling when greetings go awkwardly. Adults can model greeting behavior through puppets, showing what good greetings look like. Children can practice being both the greeter and the greeted, building understanding of both sides of the exchange. The play-based context makes repetitive practice feel like fun rather than work. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Any puppets or stuffed animals work. Create simple greeting scenarios. Take turns being different characters. Make puppets wave, say hello, respond. Use the child's favorite stuffed animals for engagement. Safety: Follow the child's lead in play. If certain scenarios cause distress, step back. Keep practice positive and pressure-free.

mainEntity.name: How does a Video Modeling Resources help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Video modeling shows children exactly what successful greetings look like in action. Watching videos of same-age peers greeting — arriving at school and saying good morning, waving to friends, saying hello to relatives — provides clear, repeatable examples. Children can watch the same video multiple times, noticing details they missed before. Video modeling has strong evidence base for teaching social skills, particularly for children on the autism spectrum. Self-video modeling, where the child watches edited videos of themselves greeting successfully, is even more powerful — they see themselves doing the skill correctly, which builds belief that they can do it. Video can pause, rewind, and replay in ways live demonstration cannot. It provides consistent, patient modeling without human variability. Price: ₹0–2,000. DIY option: Record family members or friends demonstrating greetings. Create simple greeting videos at home. Record the child when they greet successfully and play back. Use phone or tablet for viewing. Safety: Keep videos short and focused. Avoid overwhelming with too much information. Screen time should be purposeful and limited.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Cue Cards for Greeting Moments help with greeting skills deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Visual cue cards provide just-in-time reminders when greeting situations are about to occur or are occurring. A small card showing a waving hand, eye contact, or the word 'hi' can be held up as a subtle prompt, replacing repeated verbal reminders. Cue cards are less intrusive than verbal prompts, which can feel embarrassing or call attention to the child's difficulty. A quick visual signal reminds without drawing others' attention. Cue cards also help children recognize that a greeting moment is happening — the card itself signals 'now is a greeting time.' Over time, the visual cue can be faded as the child internalizes the skill. Some children carry their own cue cards in pockets or on lanyards, giving them ownership of their reminder system. Price: ₹50–300. DIY option: Create small cards with simple images: waving hand, eyes, speech bubble with 'hi.' Laminate for durability. Keep in pocket or on lanyard for easy access. Practice the cue system at home first. Safety: Cue cards should be used supportively, not punitively. Never use the card as a consequence or with frustration. Fade the cue as skill develops.

The skill involves recognition (knowing a greeting moment is occurring), understanding (grasping why greetings matter), execution (knowing what to say/do), and management (handling any discomfort). Materials and strategies that address each component systematically can build greeting skills from non-existent to natural.

citation.name: Research on Social Communication Development and Pragmatic Language Intervention
citation.description: Research in social communication development indicates that greeting skills, while appearing automatic to most neurotypical individuals, require the integration of multiple cognitive and social abilities. Children with autism spectrum disorder, social communication differences, anxiety disorders, or pragmatic language difficulties often need explicit instruction in greeting skills. Evidence-based interventions include social stories, video modeling, visual supports, structured practice, and generalization across contexts.

item.name: Video Modeling Resources
item.description: Video modeling shows children exactly what successful greetings look like in action. Watching videos of same-age peers greeting — arriving at school and saying good morning, waving to friends, saying hello to relatives — provides clear, repeatable examples. Children can watch the same video multiple times, noticing details they missed before. Video modeling has strong evidence base for teaching social skills, particularly for children on the autism spectrum. Self-video modeling, where the child watches edited videos of themselves greeting successfully, is even more powerful — they see themselves doing the skill correctly, which builds belief that they can do it. Video can pause, rewind, and replay in ways live demonstration cannot. It provides consistent, patient modeling without human variability.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Record family members or friends demonstrating greetings. Create simple greeting videos at home. Record the child when they greet successfully and play back. Use phone or tablet for viewing.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep videos short and focused. Avoid overwhelming with too much information. Screen time should be purposeful and limited.

item.name: Visual Cue Cards for Greeting Moments
item.description: Visual cue cards provide just-in-time reminders when greeting situations are about to occur or are occurring. A small card showing a waving hand, eye contact, or the word 'hi' can be held up as a subtle prompt, replacing repeated verbal reminders. Cue cards are less intrusive than verbal prompts, which can feel embarrassing or call attention to the child's difficulty. A quick visual signal reminds without drawing others' attention. Cue cards also help children recognize that a greeting moment is happening — the card itself signals 'now is a greeting time.' Over time, the visual cue can be faded as the child internalizes the skill. Some children carry their own cue cards in pockets or on lanyards, giving them ownership of their reminder system.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create small cards with simple images: waving hand, eyes, speech bubble with 'hi.' Laminate for durability. Keep in pocket or on lanyard for easy access. Practice the cue system at home first.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Cue cards should be used supportively, not punitively. Never use the card as a consequence or with frustration. Fade the cue as skill develops.

item.name: Greeting Games and Interactive Activities
item.description: Greeting games turn skill-building into play, providing repetitive practice in engaging contexts. Games might involve greeting different characters, racing to wave at picture cards, earning points for eye contact, or playing out scenarios with board game mechanics. The game structure makes the practice inherently motivating — children want to play, and greeting practice becomes part of the fun. Games also provide natural repetition without the fatigue of drill-based practice. Playing greeting games with peers or siblings normalizes the practice and adds social elements. Some games specifically target greeting skills; any game can incorporate greeting practice (greet each player before the game starts, greet characters in the game, practice greetings as part of role-play within the game).

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple greeting games: 'Greeting Bingo' with different greeting scenarios, 'Hello Hunt' scavenger games, 'Wave Race' timing games. Build greetings into existing games the child likes.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Games should be fun first. If the child is not enjoying the game, switch approaches. Avoid making games feel like tests.

item.name: Greeting Reward Charts and Visual Trackers
item.description: Reward charts and visual trackers make progress visible and celebrate greeting successes. For children who need external motivation while building intrinsic skill, charts provide immediate positive feedback. A sticker for waving at the teacher, a check mark for saying hello to grandma, a stamp for greeting independently — each success is acknowledged and recorded. Visual trackers also help children see their own growth over time, building confidence as completed greetings accumulate. Charts can track specific goals: unprompted greetings, eye contact during greetings, greeting new people. The visual record transforms abstract progress into concrete achievement. When charts lead to earned rewards — special activities, small treats, accumulated points — the greeting behavior becomes associated with positive outcomes, strengthening motivation during the difficult learning phase.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple charts with spaces for stickers or stamps. Track specific goals (morning greeting to teacher, hello to family members). Celebrate milestones. Personalize with the child's interests.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Focus on effort and progress, not perfection. Never take away earned rewards. Fade rewards gradually as skill becomes more intrinsic.

item.name: Feeling Faces and Emotion Cards
item.description: Feeling faces and emotion cards connect greetings to their emotional impact — helping children understand that greetings make people feel happy, acknowledged, and connected, while ignoring greetings can make people feel hurt, invisible, or unimportant. Many children who struggle with greetings don't fully grasp this emotional dimension. Emotion cards make the impact visible: 'When you say hello, your teacher feels happy. When you walk past, she feels sad.' This perspective-taking builds motivation for greeting that rule-following cannot create. Children who understand that their hello actually affects how someone else feels often become more willing to greet. Emotion cards can also help children identify their own feelings about greeting situations — acknowledging that greetings feel scary or hard, validating the difficulty while still building the skill.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple cards with happy, sad, and other emotion faces. Draw or photograph expressions. Discuss which face matches different scenarios: 'How does Grandma feel when you say hi? How does she feel when you walk away?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Avoid using emotion cards to induce guilt. The goal is understanding, not shame. Keep the tone curious and educational, not punitive.

socialMedia.caption: She won't even say hello — when your child ignores greetings, looks away when people speak to them, and needs prompting for every single hi, it's not rudeness. It's often a skill that hasn't been learned the way they need to learn it. Here are 9 materials that help: social stories, greeting picture cards, mirror practice, puppets for role-play, video modeling, visual cue cards, greeting games, reward charts, and feeling faces. Teaching the skill that opens every social door. Save for building greetings! 🔖 #TeachingGreetings #SocialSkills #PinnacleBlooms
